summary: Apple and Amazon have struck a deal allowing Prime Video to process in-app purchases directly through Amazon for existing Prime subscribers, bypassing Apple's In-App Purchase system entirely. For non-Prime users, purchases go through Apple's standard IAP, and users can even subscribe to Prime Video via iTunes. Gruber details the mechanics of how billing works across different subscription states and platforms, noting this is effectively unprecedented despite Apple claiming the program already existed for obscure providers like Altice One and Canal+. The deal represents a quid pro quo: Amazon built full integration with Apple's TV ecosystem (AirPlay 2, universal search, Siri, Up Next), and in return gets to process its own transactions for existing subscribers. Gruber argues it's a win for both companies and for users, with Amazon gaining new subscriber channels and Apple gaining revenue it previously got none of.
key_points: Existing Prime subscribers can now buy and rent movies directly through Amazon's payment system in the iOS/tvOS app, with Apple getting no cut — effectively unprecedented for digital content in the App Store.
Non-Prime users can subscribe to Prime Video as an iTunes subscription ($9/month) and their purchases go through Apple's IAP, giving Apple recurring revenue where it previously had none.
The quid pro quo requires Amazon to support every Apple TV ecosystem feature: AirPlay 2, tvOS app, universal search, Siri, single sign-on, and TV app integration with Up Next.
Amazon likely negotiated better terms than Apple's standard 70/30 split, similar to how Netflix previously had an 85/15 split from year one.
For Amazon, offering iTunes subscriptions is pure upside — existing Prime subscribers won't cancel to resubscribe through Apple, while new users Apple brings in represent incremental growth.
money_quotes: Apple's insistence that all in-app purchases of digital content go through Apple's official In-App Purchase mechanism — giving Apple its significant cut of each transaction — has been so steadfast ever since the inception of the App Store in 2008 that many observers genuinely wondered if Amazon had gone rogue yesterday.

But this is completely sanctioned by Apple. If you have an existing Prime account — one you signed up for at Amazon's website — you can now purchase and rent movies and TV shows in the Prime Video app directly through Amazon. Apple doesn't see a dime.
Prior to this deal, Apple made nothing from Prime Video — it was a free app with no in-app purchases, and there was no way to subscribe to Prime Video through iTunes.

If their new sign-ups for Prime are tapering off in the U.S. — which seems very possible, given how popular Prime is and how long it's been around — any new subscriptions they can get through in-app iTunes subscriptions may well be worth the recurring cut Apple will take.

There's practically zero risk that any existing Prime Video subscribers are going to cancel just to resubscribe using iTunes, and even less risk that any full-fledged Prime subscribers would downgrade to Prime Video only. It's all upside for Amazon, even with Apple's cut of in-app transactions.
It's a win for Apple, a win for Amazon, and a win for users in the Apple TV ecosystem.
Amazon has, effectively, pulled a reverse Netflix.
key_insight: Apple's rigid App Store payment rules are more flexible than they appear — when a partner offers enough ecosystem value, Apple will cut a deal that gives both sides revenue they weren't getting before.
